name: Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop
name_original: Oviedo a Trubia — circular por el vía verde Oviedo-Fuso de la Reina
description: Cycle the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop, a difficult 26.5-mile route with 2898 feet of gain and river views.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Oviedo, Spain, offers diverse landscapes ranging from mountainous terrain to scenic river valleys. The region is characterized by the foothills of Mount Naranco, the Nora and Trubia river trails, and extensive greenways. These features provide a rich blend of historical routes and natural beauty for cyclists. Best touring cycling routes around Oviedo The most popular touring cycling route is Oviedo to Fuso de la Reina on the Via Verde — circular route, a 30.1 miles (48.5 km) trail that takes 3 hours 24 minutes to complete. This difficult route offers varied terrain along a dedicated greenway. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop, a difficult 26.5 miles (42.7 km) path. This route provides extensive river vistas and passes through varied landscapes. Local touring cyclists also love the Parcoscuro Greenway – View of the Nalón River loop from Llamaquique, a 14.0 miles (22.5 km) trail leading through river valleys and green spaces, often completed in about 1 hour 30 minutes. Touring cycling around Oviedo is defined by mountainous foothills, scenic river valleys, and dedicated greenways. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate paths to challenging climbs. The routes in Oviedo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 180 reviews. More than 3,700 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Oviedo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Oviedo?
A: Oviedo offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 160 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, from moderate paths to challenging climbs through diverse landscapes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes near Oviedo?
A: Touring cycling around Oviedo features diverse terrain. You'll find routes through the mountainous foothills of Mount Naranco, scenic river valleys like the Nora and Trubia, and dedicated greenways. The region offers a rich blend of historical paths and natural beauty.

Q: Are there challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists in Oviedo?
A: Yes, Oviedo provides challenging routes for experienced touring cyclists. The region is known for its mountainous terrain, including the foothills of Mount Naranco and routes that offer significant ascents. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029990790/oviedo-to-fuso-de-la-reina-on-the-via-verde-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oviedo to Fuso de la Reina on the Via Verde — circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 30.1-mile trail with considerable elevation gain. The mythical Angliru climb is also accessible for those seeking a significant challenge in the wider Asturian mountains.

Q: Are there any easy or moderate touring cycling routes suitable for beginners around Oviedo?
A: Yes, Oviedo offers several easy and moderate touring cycling routes. While many routes are classified as difficult, there are 5 easy and 35 moderate options available. Routes along river valleys and greenways, such as the Nora River trail, are generally flatter and more accessible. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38204392" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parcoscuro Greenway – View of the Nalón River loop from Llamaquique&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 14.0-mile trail leading through river valleys and green spaces.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes around Oviedo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Oviedo are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Examples include the popular &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029990790/oviedo-to-fuso-de-la-reina-on-the-via-verde-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oviedo to Fuso de la Reina on the Via Verde — circular route&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1029991456/oviedo-fuso-de-la-reina-greenway-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop&lt;/a&gt;, both offering extensive river vistas and varied landscapes.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I see along touring cycling routes in Oviedo?
A: Oviedo's touring cycling routes often pass by significant historical and cultural landmarks. You can encounter elements of the Camino de Santiago, including ancient monasteries and medieval towns. Within Oviedo, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4395523" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of San Salvador&lt;/a&gt; is a notable sight. Routes also lead to pre-Romanesque monuments like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1070128" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint Mary of Naranco&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1070132" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of San Miguel de Lillo&lt;/a&gt;, both UNESCO World Heritage sites. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/210761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sacred Heart of Jesus Monument on Monte Naranco&lt;/a&gt; also offers panoramic views.

Q: Are there natural attractions like waterfalls or scenic viewpoints on touring cycling routes near Oviedo?
A: Yes, the region around Oviedo boasts beautiful natural attractions. You can find routes that pass through forests and lead to waterfalls, such as the Guanga Waterfalls near San Andrés de Trubia. Many routes also offer stunning scenic viewpoints, particularly those ascending Mount Naranco, where you can enjoy vistas from places like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2923226" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Naranco&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/210761" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sacred Heart of Jesus Monument&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Oviedo?
A: The touring cycling routes in Oviedo are highly regarded by the komoot community, with an average rating of 4.4 stars from over 180 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, the well-maintained greenways, and the blend of natural beauty with historical and cultural points of interest. Over 3,700 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, highlighting its popularity and quality.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Oviedo?
A: Oviedo offers pleasant conditions for touring cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide mild temperatures and beautiful scenery, making them ideal. Summer can also be enjoyable, especially on routes that offer shade or lead to coastal areas. It's always advisable to check the local weather forecast before heading out.

Q: Is public transport accessible for cyclists to reach trailheads in Oviedo?
A: Oviedo's public transport system, including local buses and regional trains, can often accommodate cyclists, though policies may vary. For specific routes or longer distances, regional trains are generally more cyclist-friendly for reaching trailheads further afield. It's recommended to check with local transport providers for their current regulations regarding bicycles.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes near Oviedo?
A: Parking is generally available near many popular touring cycling trailheads around Oviedo. For routes like the Fuso de la Reina Greenway or the Nora River Trail, designated parking areas can often be found at key access points. Within Oviedo itself, various public parking facilities are available, from which you can cycle to nearby route starts. Always look for official parking signs and consider local regulations.

Q: Are there cafes or restaurants along the touring cycling routes in Oviedo?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes around Oviedo pass through or near charming towns and villages where you can find cafes and restaurants. Oviedo itself is known for its vibrant gastronomy, particularly along the famous cider boulevard, offering plenty of options for a post-ride meal. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40007663" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manjoya Route – Medieval Tower of Proaza loop from Llamaquique&lt;/a&gt; will take you through areas where you can find local eateries.
tips: This greenway connects Oviedo and Fuso de la Reina with good paving on which you can walk quietly appreciating the green landscape. The route crosses the winter park of the Asturian capital.

A great place full of trees, fountains, statues and flower gardens. Visit the Palacio de Velázquez, an impressive building located in the park that was once a royal residence. Today it houses the Museum of Fine Arts of Asturias, where you can see a collection of art from the Middle Ages to the present day.
One of the most popular statues in the park is the Statue of Mafalda, a character from a popular Argentine comic strip. The statue is a tribute to the creator of the comic, Quino, born in Argentina but with Asturian roots.
You can also visit the Oviedo Botanical Garden: Located a few steps from the park, it has more than 30,000 plants, including many rare and endangered species.

This is where the camino primitivo actually begins.

Oviedo Cathedral is the main church of the Archdiocese of Oviedo. It is dedicated to the Redeemer (San Salvador). Other churches and buildings belong to the complex.

Today's cathedral had at least three previous buildings: the high medieval churches of King Fruela I from the 8th century and of King Alfonso II from the 9th century and their late medieval, Romanesque successor.

The Asturian king Fruela I arranged for the construction of the first church dedicated to San Salvador on the site where the cathedral is today. The exact time for this is unknown. In an inscription destroyed in the 16th century, which his son Alfonso II made and which Bishop Pelayo (1098–1129) recorded in the cathedral's documentary book, Fruela I is attested as the founder. This first church was destroyed in a Muslim raid in 794. Nothing is known about the architecture of this first church.

Source: Wikipedia
Oviedo's old town is full of beautiful architecture, historic buildings and charming streets. Be sure to visit Plaza del Fontán, Calle Gascona and Plaza de la Catedral.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking near the start of the greenway in Oviedo, such as around the Winter Park area. For a convenient starting point, consider parking near Campo de San Francisco, which is close to the city center and the greenway's beginning.

faqs.question: Is the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: The core Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina greenway section (approximately 8 km one-way) is largely flat, paved, and considered easy to moderate, making it suitable for beginners, families, and those with reduced mobility. However, the full 42.7 km loop, which extends beyond the greenway, is rated as difficult due to significant elevation gains (around 880m) and requires very good fitness.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The primary section of the greenway is predominantly paved, wide, and asphalted, offering a smooth surface for cycling. The route also features unique elements like four tunnels and at least one viaduct, adding to the experience. While the greenway itself is well-maintained, be aware that longer circular routes incorporating it might include unpaved or more challenging segments.

faqs.question: What can I see along the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop?
faqs.answer: The route transitions from urban settings near Oviedo Old Town and the Cathedral of San Salvador in Oviedo to lush natural landscapes. You'll cycle along the banks of the Nalón River, through dense forests, past old mills, and agricultural areas. Keep an eye out for spectacular views of the Nalón River, holm oak groves, and distant peaks like Mostayal. The route itself, the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway, is a highlight.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Asturias, known as the 'Green Coast,' offers beautiful scenery year-round. However, for the most comfortable cycling experience, spring (April-June) and early autumn (September-October) are ideal, offering pleasant temperatures and vibrant landscapes. Summers can be warm, and winters might bring cooler, wetter conditions.

faqs.question: Is the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, greenways in Spain, including this one, are generally dog-friendly. Dogs are usually permitted on a leash. Always ensure your dog is under control and that you clean up after them to maintain the trail's cleanliness for all users.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to access and cycle the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway Loop. It is a public recreational path, freely accessible to everyone.

faqs.question: Does this trail connect to other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina Greenway is part of a larger network of Asturian greenways. It serves as a connector to other significant routes, including sections of the Vía Verde Oviedo - Fuso de la Reina, the Camino Natural del Rio Naló, and the Senda Verde Fuso de la Reina - Tuñon. It can also be linked to the more extensive Senda del Oso (Bear Trail).

faqs.question: Are there places to rent bicycles near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Yes, as Oviedo is a popular starting point for cycling routes, you can find several bicycle rental shops within the city. It's advisable to book in advance, especially during peak season, to ensure availability.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes or facilities along the route?
faqs.answer: While the greenway itself is primarily natural, you'll find facilities and cafes in Oviedo at the start. Along the route, especially in towns like Fuso de la Reina or Trubia (if you do the full loop), there are opportunities for refreshments. The old La Manjoya station, now an environmental education center, can also be a point of interest.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I encounter on the greenway?
faqs.answer: The greenway passes through diverse natural habitats, offering opportunities to spot various wildlife. You might see different bird species, squirrels, and if you're lucky, even wild boar or Egyptian vultures, especially in the more secluded, forested sections along the Nalón River.
